coredata.dc:title: l-proline covered N doped graphene quantum dots modified CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposite as a robust retrievable catalyst in synthesis of substituted chiral 2-amino-4H-chromenes
coredata.dc:creator: Babaei, Pouria
Safaei-Ghomi, Javad
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen-doped Graphene Quantum Dots (N-GQDs) is a significant carbon nanomaterial that can be used to design various metal-organocatalyst. Modification of N-GQDs plays a serious role in catalytic activity, recyclability, thermal stability, and so on. Also, finding revealed that homogeneity, morphology, and size of nanocomposites can be intensively influenced via various procedures and precursors. Hence, CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites with morphological engineering have been prepared by the hydrothermal method for improving performance. On the other hand, the study of the effect of amino acid as a chiral organic group on the surface of modified N-GQDs is essential for expanding new inorganic-organic hybrid catalysts. In this work, we introduced l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs hexagonal nanocomposites as a robust recyclable nanocatalyst in multicomponent reactions (MCRs) for the preparation of 2-amino-4H-chromenes. As-prepared CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs@l-proline nanocatalyst has been investigated by High-resolution Transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), Transmission electron microscope (TEM), Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET), Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD). High atom economy (95%), recyclability catalyst (8 runs), avoided the utilization of further reagents such as additives, and excellent yield of products (80–94%) are a few vital highlights of this method.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
CuO/ZnO nanocomposites
Microwave irradiation
2-Amino-4H-chromenes
Multicomponent reactions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Typical procedure for the synthesis of 2-amino-4H-chromenes
Typical method for the synthesis of 2-amino-4H-chromenes in microwave conditions
Physical and spectroscopic data of new and selected products
Comparison of the efficiency of solvents, amount of CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs@l-proline nanocomposites, and other catalysts on the synthesis of 2-amino-4H-chromenes
preparation of 2-amino-4H-chromenes by using CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs@l-proline nanocomposites at room temperature
A plausible mechanism for the preparation of 2-amino-4H-chromenes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by the University of Kashan . The authors especially thank the department of organic chemistry.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns for a) CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites obtained at 150 °C for 12 h, b) l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites.
The EDS spectrum of samples a) CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites, b) l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites.
The comparative FT-IR spectra of a) CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites, b) CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites, c) l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites.
N2 adsorption−desorption isotherms of (a) CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites, (b) l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites.
TG analysis of l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposite.
FE-SEM images of CuO/ZnO nanocomposites in 120 °C, at 12 h and with mole ratio a) 1:1, b) 1:2, c) 1:3.
FE-SEM images of CuO/ZnO nanocomposites in 150 °C, at 12 h and with mole ratio a) 1:1, b) 1:2, c) 1:3.
FE-SEM images of CuO/ZnO nanocomposites in 180 °C, at 12 h and with mole ratio a) 1:1, b) 1:2, c) 1:3.
FE-SEM images of a,b) CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites in 150 °C, at 12 h and with mole ratio:1:6 (Cu2+: Zn2+), c) CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites, d) l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites.
(a) TEM image and (b–d) HRTEM of CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposite.
XRD graph of l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites after 8 reaction cycles.
l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites as a retrievable catalyst in the synthesis of 2-amino-4H-chromene derivatives.
Illustration of the preparation of l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposite.
Schematic interaction of l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs hexagonal nanocomposite.
Proposed mechanism for the synthesis of chromenes in presence of l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites.
Various conditions of synthesis procedure CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites in pH = 12.
Structural and Textural properties of nanocatalyst.
Optimization of reaction conditions using various solvents and catalysts.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: 4-nitrobenzaldehyde (1 mmol), malononitrile (1 mmol), and dimedone (1 mmol).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Recycling of l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites as the catalyst.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: a
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: 4-nitrobenzaldehyde (1 mmol), malononitrile (1 mmol), and dimedone (1 mmol) in presence of 10 mg catalyst.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of synthesis of chromenes using l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposite (10 mg) under MW irradiation and reflux conditions .

head.ce:title: l-proline covered N doped graphene quantum dots modified CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposite as a robust retrievable catalyst in synthesis of substituted chiral 2-amino-4H-chromenes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Organic Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Kashan, Kashan, 51167, I. R. Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Organic Chemistry, Faculty of Chemistry, University of Kashan, Kashan, 51167, I. R. Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen-doped Graphene Quantum Dots (N-GQDs) is a significant carbon nanomaterial that can be used to design various metal-organocatalyst. Modification of N-GQDs plays a serious role in catalytic activity, recyclability, thermal stability, and so on. Also, finding revealed that homogeneity, morphology, and size of nanocomposites can be intensively influenced via various procedures and precursors. Hence, CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites with morphological engineering have been prepared by the hydrothermal method for improving performance. On the other hand, the study of the effect of amino acid as a chiral organic group on the surface of modified N-GQDs is essential for expanding new inorganic-organic hybrid catalysts. In this work, we introduced l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs hexagonal nanocomposites as a robust recyclable nanocatalyst in multicomponent reactions (MCRs) for the preparation of 2-amino-4H-chromenes. As-prepared CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs@l-proline nanocatalyst has been investigated by High-resolution Transmission electron microscope (HRTEM), Transmission electron microscope (TEM), Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET), Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA), and X-ray diffraction analysis (XRD). High atom economy (95%), recyclability catalyst (8 runs), avoided the utilization of further reagents such as additives, and excellent yield of products (80–94%) are a few vital highlights of this method.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) with diameters of multiple nanometers are graphene fragments and belong to a novel class of carbon nanomaterials [1–3]. Unlike other families of carbon nanostructures, these nanostructures exhibit amazing attributes, for instance, 1) an excellent fluorescence, high specific surface area [4], 2) great solubility and exceedingly stable dispersion in water [5,6], 3) can be prepared various functional groups like hydroxyl and carboxyl at outside the edge of structures [7] and 4) according to the efficiency requirements. These structures can be adjusted easily by N-, S- or N, S-doping [8,9]. Up to the present, various procedures have been reported for the synthesis of GQDs such as microwave, pyrolysis in concentrated acid, hydrothermal, electrochemical cutting, microwave-assisted cutting, and ultrasonic shearing, etc. [10,11]. Moreover, the growth of controllable, scalable, easily obtained, and eco-friendly fabrication of GQDs is significant for basic research and potential applications in catalysis [12,13]. Consequently, due to the chemically active sites on the surface of N-GQDs skeletons, these structures are used as the anchoring sites for metal particles immobilization, nanocomposites, chemical functionalization, and so on [14,15]. It is worth noting that ZnO-based nanostructures are one the best options for modification of N-GQDs owing to widely attributes (low cost [16], biocompatibility [17], and high electron mobility [18]), and applications in different fields such as semiconductor-based industry, water splitting, and cosmetic industry [19,20]. Various elements can dope ZnO-based nanostructures. Among them, CuO/ZnO nanocomposites have special consideration owing to their various applications such as catalysts in organic synthesis, solar cell, photocatalyst, and so on [21,22].

l-proline has been recognized as a remarkable chiral small-molecule [23]. It is a unique secondary proteinogenic amino acid with particular rigidity controlling the stereochemistry [24]. Low cost, eco-friendly [25], and easy access [26] lead to the use of this proteinogenic amino acid as a chiral catalyst in organic synthesis for its enantioselective or diastereoselective via multicomponent reactions (MCRs) [27,28]. On the other hand, prolonged reaction time and difficult recovery are the disadvantages of this catalyst [29]. There are some strategies for resolving their drawbacks such as the addition of additives [30], co-catalysts, and various types of support like polymers [31,32], ionic liquid (IL) [33], and Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [34,35]. In modern days, Microwave irradiation (MW) is demonstrating to be a profitable technique in organic synthesis. This strategy has superseded the conventional heating method owing to deep and rapid heating, reproducible path, excellent yield, and short reaction time [36–38].

The derivative of chromenes, which are a vital category of oxygen-containing ring structures, appear in natural products and pharmaceutically active products [39,40]. In particular, because of their diverse scope of pharmacological and biological properties, for instances anti-inflammatory [41], antimicrobial [42], antibacterial, antioxidant, anti-HIV [15,43], diuretic [44], anticancer and anti-anaphylactic activities [45,46], the preparation of 2-amino-4H-chromenes are taken into consideration by investigators for medicinal applications. In such a manner, looking for efficient ways for the synthesis of chromenes is an exciting challenge. A few strategies have been proposed for the one-pot synthesis of 2-amino-4H-chromene derivatives within the participation of various catalysts, including DBU [47], nano-sized MgO and CuO particles [48,49], [2-Eim]OAc [50], and (N, N-dimethylaminoethyl) benzyldimethylammonium chloride [51]. It should be emphasized that the reported approaches for the synthesis of various substituted 2-amino-4H-chromenes suffered from numerous inherent drawbacks like low yields and recyclable [52], not mild reaction conditions [53], longer reaction times [54]. One of the powerful tools and impressive methods for preparing chromenes and their derivatives is MCRs [55].

In the contribution of our investigations on efficient catalysts and green organic synthesis, we for the first time demonstrate a facile one-pot approach to the synthesis of chromenes and their derivatives in the presence of l-proline-assisted N-GQDs-decorated CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites as a new generation of a retrievable heterogeneous catalyst. As illustrated in Scheme 1 , 2-amino-4H-chromene derivatives are synthesized through a three-component reactions approach of malononitrile, dimedone, and various aromatic aldehydes in the presence of l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites under MW conditions.

All melting points were determined by an Electro-Thermal 9200 and are uncorrected. FT-IR spectra of all compounds were recorded on Nicolet Magna 550 IR spectrometer using KBr pellets in the scope of 400–4000 cm−1. XRD pattern was recorded on a Philips diffractometer using monochromatized Cu Kα radiation (k = 1.5406 Å). FE-SEM images of products were imagined by an LEO instrument (model 1455VP). 13C and 1H NMR spectra were measured by a Bruker 400 MHz spectrometer at 100 and 400 MHz, respectively. TMS is an internal standard and was carried out using CDCl3 as a NMR spectroscopy solvent. The morphology and size distribution of CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs were taken by High-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, FEI Tecnai F20). The elemental analyses (N, C, and H) were recorded applying a Carlo ERBA analyzer (model EA1108). The entire chemicals were purchased in analytical grade from Merck and Sigma-Aldrich without extra purification.

CuO/ZnO nanocomposites have been prepared via the one-pot hydrothermal route. In this process, the calculated amount of each copper acetate and zinc acetate were dissolved in ultrapure water (7 ml) separately; later, the zinc acetate solution was added to the copper acetate solution dropwise. Previously prepared NaOH(aq) (3 M) was added drop by drop. The solution directly got to be opaque after addition caustic soda and stirred for 15 min at 25 °C. The whole mixture, thereafter, was transferred to a 150 ml Teflon Lined stainless steel autoclave. The molar ratio and various temperature conditions are listed in Table 1 . The dark sediment, finally, was centrifuged and followed by washing with distilled water and ethanol frequently. The separate precipitate, finally, dried at 65 °C and calcined at 500 °C for 2 h to achieve a powder sample.

Previous reports on fabrication of CuO/ZnO nanocomposites show the following chemical equation [56]:
Cu(CH3CO2)2 + Zn(CH3CO2)2 + 4 NaOH → CuO/ZnO + 4 NaCH3CO2 + 4H2O

Initially, ethylenediamine (0.5 ml), citric acid (1.2 g), and ultrapure water (50 ml) were stirred for about 2 min at room temperature, followed by the addition of calcined CuO/ZnO nanocomposites (0.20 g). After well mixing, the mixture was placed in 150 ml Teflon Lined stainless steel autoclave. The sealed autoclave was put on an electric oven for the hydrothermal reaction for 9 h at 180 °C. At completion, the dark brown mixture was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for 30 min. The separate precipitate, finally, dried at 60 °C for 24 h under vacuum conditions.

Briefly, 0.6 g of l-proline was dissolved in dried toluene (15 ml) with CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites (1 g) in refluxing conditions and under N2 atmosphere. Next, the obtained residue was centrifuged and separated, followed by washing with dry EtOH three times. Finally, the purified precipitate was dried overnight at 50 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Typical procedure for the synthesis of 2-amino-4H-chromenes
ce:section.ce:para: To a round bottom, the flask was added 1 mmol aromatic aldehydes (1), 1 mmol malononitrile (2), 1 mmol dimedone (3), or 4-hydroxycoumarin (5), CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs@l-proline nanocomposites catalyst (10 mg), and acetonitrile (6 ml). The whole mixture subsequently was stirred for the time appropriate at room temperature. TLC was used to monitor the progress of the reaction. At completion, the precipitate reaction was collected and washed with cold EtOH several times and dried.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Typical method for the synthesis of 2-amino-4H-chromenes in microwave conditions
ce:section.ce:para: To a round bottom, the flask was added 1 mmol aromatic aldehydes (1), 1 mmol malononitrile (2), 1 mmol dimedone (3), or 4-hydroxycoumarine (5), CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs@l-proline nanocomposites catalyst (10 mg), and acetonitrile (6 ml). The whole mixture subsequently was stirred for 1 min at room temperature. After that, the mixture was exposed to microwave irradiation at 750 W. Upon completion of the time reaction (monitored by TLC), the crude product was washed with cold EtOH several times to get pure product. All products were characterized by IR, 1H, 13C NMR spectroscopy, and CHNS analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Physical and spectroscopic data of new and selected products

ce:section.ce:para: Upon completion of the time reaction, chloroform was add owing to the insolubility of nanocomposite and hence it could be recycled by centrifuge at a speed of 12000 rpm for 5 min. Then collected on a filter, washed with EtOH and water several times, and dried at 60 °C for 6 h.

In recent decades, heterogeneous catalysts and also heterostructures composed of binary oxides have especially been considered in organic synthesis. Among of series of nanocomposite systems, CuO/ZnO nanocomposites have attracted much consideration and have been explored for different applications such as catalyst [57]. CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites have been effectively prepared by hydrothermal methodology (Scheme 2 ).

GQDs owing to high-specific surface area and hydrophilic surface functional groups (OH and CO2H) on the edge of sheets play a significant role in nanocomposite coatings. Moreover, the N doped actuates a positive charge on the carbon atoms present in the GQDs owing to more electronegativity of N (3.04) than that of carbon (2.55) atoms [58]. The functionalization of N-GQDs happens through hydrogen bonding between l-proline and N-GQDs (Scheme 3 ).

Fig. 1 depicts the XRD patterns of l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites. All diffraction peaks displayed in Fig. 1a illustrated that as-prepared CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites (viz. calcined CuO/ZnO) were composed. The diffraction angles (2θ) for CuO are 35.60°, 39.15°, 49.22°, and 58.23°, and also the diffraction angles (2θ) for ZnO include 32.05°, 34.72°, 36.54°, 48.33°, 56.87°, 61.83°, 66.33°, 68.28°, and 75.48°, which following standard CuO and ZnO XRD patterns (JCPDS card No. 80–1268 and 80–0075, respectively). Miller indices for CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites are attached in Fig. 1a. It becomes apparent that the prepared nanocomposites only have two crystalline phases correspond to CuO and ZnO. Crystallite sizes were calculated from Scherrer equation (Dc = Kλ/(βCosθ)), where β is the line broadening at half the maximum intensity (FWHM), K is a dimensionless shape factor, which usually takes a value of about 0.9, and λ also is the X-ray wavelength (0.156 nm for CuKα radiation) were approximately 32 nm for CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites. From Fig. 1b, it can be viewed as the presence of a broad peak at 2θ = 23° relating to the (002) plane can be related to the disordered structure of N-GQDs [59]. Moreover, the peaks obtained at 2θ = 44.1°, 52.9°, and 74.3° relating to (400), (422), and (440) planes, respectively, shows that the crystallite structure of the CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs@l-proline nanoparticles is formed [60].

Next, we studied the EDS spectroscopy for the inquiry purity of the synthesized CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites after calcination (Fig. 2 ). The results of the EDS analysis revealed that all the prepared CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites have consisted of the elements of oxygen, copper, and zinc (51.61 w/w%, 35.35 w/w%, and 13.04 w/w%, respectively) (Fig. 2a). Then, the CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposite was covered by N-GQDs and functionalized by l-proline. The final structure of l-proline-assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposite was confirmed by EDS analysis results, as shown in Fig. 2b.

The specific areas of CuO/ZnO and l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites were determined by Brunauer–Emmett–Teller method (BET). The measurements were performed with a Belsorp mini II device at the boiling temperature of liquid nitrogen (−196 °C). Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms are displayed in Fig. 4 . According to these patterns, the specific surface area (a
s , BET ) is improved from 0.16 (m2/g) to 2.73 (m2/g) after modification with the coating with N-GQDs and supported by l-proline. Hence, more active sites were introduced to the l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocatalyst. The results for N2 adsorption/desorption containing the BET surface area (S
a, BET ) and the total pore volumes (V
total ) of the calcined CuO/ZnO, CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs, and l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocatalyst are summarized in Table 2 .

TGA was utilized to assess the thermal behavior of the l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites. Therefore, TG analysis was applied on a Mettler TG50 analyzer under the inert condition in the scope 30–800 °C and at a heating rate of 10 °C.min−1. Thermal analysis of l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites as a final product was presented in Fig. 5 . Results reveal appropriate thermal stability without weight loss. A partial diminish in the weight (3%) was recorded at a temperature from about 50 to 100 °C. This is because of removing surface solvent or –OH groups absorbed on the outside of the surface. Proportionate to temperature ascent from 100 to 550 °C, a 47% reduction in weight was observed. That probably corresponds to losing absorbed solvent molecules trapped in the N-GQDs layer and decomposing functionalized l-proline products.

Some various and essential factors such as temperature, reaction time, and mole ratio effectively affect the morphology and particle size [61]. We evaluated the morphological properties of calcined CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposite by using FE-SEM spectroscopy. These investigations were carried out under various conditions and acknowledged that CuO/ZnO nanocomposites have hexagonal structures. So, the hydrothermal route is carried out under various conditions for obtaining optimum conditions. Fig. 6 a and b reveal FE-SEM images of the prepared CuO/ZnO nanocomposites obtained at 12 h and 120 °C in various mole ratios (Cu2+: Zn2+ = 1:1 and 1:2), respectively. It clear that with increasing the amount of zinc, the formation of hexagonal nanocomposite is successful (Fig. 6c).

As shown in Fig. 7 for appropriate morphology, the temperature was changed from 120 °C to 150 °C. Fig. 7a–c showed the formation of bulk nanocomposite and hexagonal nanocomposite next to each other in various mole ratios at 150 °C. Regrettably, no impressive morphology was observed during the increasing temperature to 180 °C (Fig. 8 a and b). From Fig. 8c, increasing the amount of zinc metal causes morphology to change to hexagonal. By investigating the previous FE-SEM images, the optimized conditions for the formation of hexagonal morphology proposed 12 h and 150 °C. A mole ratio of Cu2+: Zn2+ (1:6) was prepared under optimized conditions to grow well-defined hexagonal morphology. Fig. 9 reveals the FE-SEM of calcined CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites, CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites and l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites.

Furthermore, for better determination of the morphology and size of CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs, it was clearly defined by TEM and HRTEM spectroscopy. Fig. 10 a shows TEM images of CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites that confirmed the preparation of nanocomposites. A typical HRTEM image of nanocomposites (Fig. 10(b–d)) demonstrates lattice spacings of 0.26, 0.23, and 0.16 nm, which are assigned to (111) CuO (JCPDS file No. 80–1268), (002) ZnO (JCPDS file No. 80–0075) and (002) planes of graphitic carbon (JCPDS file No. 01–0646), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of the efficiency of solvents, amount of CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs@l-proline nanocomposites, and other catalysts on the synthesis of 2-amino-4H-chromenes
ce:section.ce:para: Justification of the l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposite was explored in MCR reactions. The three-component reaction of dimedone, acetonitrile, and 4-nitrobenzaldehyde were selected as a model reaction, and results also were listed in Table 3 . No product was observed in the absence of a catalyst or the presence of an acid catalyst (CH3CO2H). We compared the influence of different catalysts, for example, NiO NPs, MgO NPs, amino acids. Also, in previous our work, catalytic performance of ZnO and CuO nanoparticles were separately investigated [62]. Synergistic role of in this work was determined as better catalytic performance. Besides, the catalytic performance of CuO/ZnO nanocomposites, CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites and CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs@l-proline nanocomposites were tested. Surprisingly, in the presence of CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs@l-proline nanocomposites (10 mg), the reaction rate and yield were increased. According to the results displayed in Table 3, the effect of different solvents such as DMF, H2O, CH3CN, and EtOH has been studied on tandem one-pot reaction (Table 3, entries 12–15). The satisfying results were viewed in terms of yield and reaction time in acetonitrile solvent at room temperature. As reported in the literature and study of results published such as Zn2SnO4–SnO2 [40], ZnO nanoparticles [42], ionic liquid [2-Eim]OAc [43], and comparison with our catalyst, it can be said that the use of N-GQDs@l-proline based on CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites as a new generation of recyclable heterogeneous catalyst and room temperature conditions ensure more efficiency for access to chiral 2-amino-4H-chromenes derivatives.

ce:section.ce:section-title: preparation of 2-amino-4H-chromenes by using CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs@l-proline nanocomposites at room temperature
ce:section.ce:para: As explained, a new hybrid of heterogeneous catalysts, reusability, chemical stability, and non-toxicity are some advantages of l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites. The effectiveness of the catalyst was tried for the preparation of 2-amino-4H-chromenes. So, by comparison, various aromatic aldehydes (see Table 5 ), when the reaction was carried out in aromatic aldehydes containing electron-withdrawing groups, the reaction rate appears, and the higher yield was observed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: A plausible mechanism for the preparation of 2-amino-4H-chromenes
ce:section.ce:para: An admissible mechanism and catalytic cycle for the synthesis of 2-amino-4H-chromenes by using l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites is illustrated in Scheme 4 . We supposed, initially, the reaction starts via the active sites (electrostatic attraction) of CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocatalyst binds with the oxygen atom of the carbonyl. Followed by the removal acidic hydrogen from malononitrile by l-proline of nanocatalyst, makes the Knoevenagel condensation between aromatic aldehyde and malononitrile. So intermediate (I) is formed. As it is well shown after that, dimedone reacts by intermediate (I) through a Michael addition to give the intermediate (II). Next, the interaction between CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs of nanocatalyst and nitrogen of the nitrile group occurs via electrostatic attraction and concurrent with the removal acidic hydrogen by l-proline of nanocatalyst. Then, the intermediate (III) is formed by an intramolecular cyclization reaction. Finally, the migration of the hydrogen atom will give the ultimate product. By comparing the previous reports [40–44], in this method, a novel generation of recyclable organometal nanocatalyst “l-proline assisted N-GQDs-decorated CuO/ZnO hexagonal nanocomposites” were used in the synthesis of 2-amino-4H-chromenes via tandem MCRs to improve efficiency in a reaction time, condition of reaction and purification.

In summary, a new generation of l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites has been introduced as a newly heterogeneous organometal nanocatalyst and also fully characterized and studied by FT-IR, FE-SEM, HRTEM, TEM, EDX, and XRD. Using N-GQDs as a substrate by a high specific surface area caused to increase in the active site of the nanocatalyst. Therefore, l-proline assisted CuO/ZnO@N-GQDs nanocomposites as an efficient nanocatalyst to synthesize 2-amino-4H-chromenes via MCRs of dimedone, malononitrile, and various aromatic aldehydes under MW irradiation. The key signs of progress of this procedure are excellent yields, facile and express synthesis, high atom economy, soft reaction conditions, and terse reaction time. Also, the use of metal matrix nanocomposites has several advantages: higher specific area, lower coefficients of thermal expansion, and better wear resistance. The advantages of this nanocatalyst are easy recovery, high recyclability, and easy work-up. According to this data, the offered procedure has a significant role in the preparation of 2-amino-4H-chromenes.

Pouria Babaei: Data curation, Formal analysis and/or interpretation of data, Writing – original draft, Revising the manuscript critically for important intellectual content, Approval of the version of the manuscript to be published (the names of all authors must be listed). Javad Safaei-Ghomi: Conceptualization, Conception and design of study, Formal analysis and/or interpretation of data, Revising the manuscript critically for important intellectual content, Approval of the version of the manuscript to be published (the names of all authors must be listed).

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by the University of Kashan. The authors especially thank the department of organic chemistry.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Corrosion resistance enhancement of magnesium alloy by N-doped graphene quantum dots and polymethyltrimethoxysilane composite coating
